periods.name: Overnight
periods.detailed: A chance of showers and thunderstorms before 2am, then a slight chance of showers and thunderstorms between 2am and 5am. Mostly cloudy, with a low around 67. South wind around 13 mph. Chance of precipitation is 50%. New rainfall amounts between a tenth and quarter of an inch possible.

periods.name: Wednesday
periods.short: Chance Showers And Thunderstorms then Mostly Sunny
periods.detailed: A chance of showers and thunderstorms between 8am and 10am, then a slight chance of showers and thunderstorms between 10am and noon. Mostly sunny, with a high near 77. Southwest wind around 13 mph. Chance of precipitation is 30%. New rainfall amounts less than a tenth of an inch possible.

periods.name: Wednesday Night
periods.detailed: Partly cloudy, with a low around 56. West wind 6 to 13 mph.

periods.name: Thursday
periods.detailed: Sunny, with a high near 79. West wind around 7 mph.

periods.name: Thursday Night
periods.detailed: Mostly clear, with a low around 58. Southwest wind around 6 mph.

periods.name: Friday
periods.detailed: Sunny, with a high near 84. South wind around 5 mph.

key_messages: Showers with some embedded thunderstorms this afternoon and evening, diminishing overnight.
Some lingering showers Wednesday as a front moves through, then becoming drier through Friday.
Quiet weather and increasing temperatures are expected this weekend before another storm system brings showers and thunderstorm chances to the region early next week.
afd.what_changed: As of 249 AM EDT Tuesday... Thunderstorm chances for Wednesday have been lowered as an earlier timing of the frontal passage allows for less destabilization.
afd.raw:
000
FXUS61 KBTV 212353
AFDBTV

Area Forecast Discussion
National Weather Service Burlington VT
753 PM EDT Tue Jul 21 2026

.WHAT HAS CHANGED...
As of 249 AM EDT Tuesday...

Thunderstorm chances for Wednesday have been lowered as an 
earlier timing of the frontal passage allows for less 
destabilization.

&&

.KEY MESSAGES...
As of 209 PM EDT Tuesday...

1.  Showers with some embedded thunderstorms this afternoon and 
evening, diminishing overnight. 

2. Some lingering showers Wednesday as a front moves through, 
then becoming drier through Friday.  

3. Quiet weather and increasing temperatures are expected this 
weekend before another storm system brings showers and thunderstorm 
chances to the region early next week.

&&

.DISCUSSION...
As of 209 PM EDT Tuesday...

KEY MESSAGE 1: Scattered showers and thunderstorms will move into the 
area this afternoon from the west, driven by shortwave energy 
rotating around the base of an upper level trough.  A longwave 
trough axis will gradually shift eastward today through eastern 
Ontario, placing our forecast area in deep southwesterly flow ahead 
of the trough. This setup will briefly drive a higher dewpoint 
airmass northward into our area this afternoon and evening, when 
PWats are forecast to rise to 1.5+ inches in valley locations. 
Satellite imagery and observations show ample cloud cover over our 
area, which is limiting heating.  Have continued to cap temperatures 
slightly lower than suggested by the NBM, with highs this afternoon 
expected to remain in the mid 70s to low 80s. These temperatures, 
along with dewpoints rising to the 60s, will allow for some marginal 
destabilization to develop.  The latest model guidance suggests 
MLCAPE values will rise to 250-750 J/kg later this afternoon. With 
no strong boundary moving through and a lack of low-level 
convergence, this afternoon's showers and thunderstorms will be 
generally be tied to more disorganized individual shortwaves moving 
through the broader flow. This leads to the best shower and 
thunderstorm chances this afternoon into this evening, when the 
individual shortwaves currently visible on water vapor 
satellite imagery over and north of NY will move into our area. 
0-6 km bulk shear values between 20 and 40 knots could support 
the development of some more organized multicell 
clusters...though overall lack of strong instability will remain
the limiting factor. Some storms today may produce gusty winds,
small hail, and locally heavy rainfall. Given the strong 
background flow with gusts 20 to 30 mph common in the Champlain 
Valley right now, it won't be difficult to get some gusts to at 
least 40 mph or higher within showers or thunderstorms. Coverage
of showers and thunderstorms will diminish after midnight 
tonight with the loss of instability and deeper moisture. 

Storm total QPF amounts for this afternoon through tonight will 
generally range from 0.25 inch near the International Border to 
0.75+ inch over the Adirondacks.  Noting some swaths of 1.0+ inch 
QPF progged in some of the higher res guidance in areas where 
heavier showers or thunderstorms track through. This does look 
like a reasonable scenario for some isolated locations based on
available moisture profiles. While 1.0+ inch rainfall totals 
will be the exception rather than the norm, the most favorable 
locations for heavier rainfall tracks will be over the southern 
St. Lawrence Valley, the Adirondacks, and potentially into 
portions of southwestern VT. Some ponding in poor drainage areas
is possible, but overall flood risk is low. 

KEY MESSAGE 2: A cold front will move through on Wednesday from
west to east, with latest guidance favoring a slightly faster 
progression of the boundary. Scattered showers and thunderstorms
can be expected morning through early afternoon as the front 
moves through, though cloud cover and the earlier timing of the 
frontal passage will limit destabilization. Though the overall 
severe threat is low, can't rule out an isolated stronger 
thunderstorm capable of briefly heavy rainfall and gusty winds.
The trend towards a lower severe threat tomorrow due to the 
earlier timing of the frontal passage is reflected in the latest
Convective Outlook update from the Storm Prediction Center. 
This outlook has much of Vermont outlined in the General Thunder
risk, and shifts the Marginal severe risk further east and out 
of our forecast area. 

High temperatures for Wednesday will be dependent on frontal timing, 
with the earlier onset of cold air advection in our western counties 
keeping highs in the 60s over the Saint Lawrence Valley and 
Adirondacks. Further east, The Champlain Valley along with 
eastern Vermont will see highs in the 70s. 

Late Wednesday through Friday will be dry with high pressure in 
control.  Humidity levels will be comfortable with highs in the 70s 
Thursday and mid 70s to mid 80s Friday.

KEY MESSAGE 3: High pressure will begin to drift east this weekend with 
more southerly to southwesterly flow, helping to advect warmer air 
into the region. Temperatures will warm into the low to mid 80s in 
general, with perhaps a few spot upper 80s in the Champlain Valley. 
Given an unfavorable moisture advection pattern, dewpoints should 
remain on the drier side for the weekend, remaining in the 50s. Warm 
air advection will keep overnight temperatures on the mild side in 
the low to mid 60s. Subsidence under the high should keep any 
precipitation at bay through most of the weekend with mainly clear 
skies. Our next system is expected to swing through the area late 
Sunday into early next week. A cyclonic gyre over northern Quebec 
will bring troughiness to the East Coast, with a few shortwaves 
riding along the southern periphery of the gyre through the Great 
Lakes into our region. There is still quite the spread in the timing 
and phasing of any of these shortwaves, but trends have denoted best 
chances for precipitation to be Monday and Tuesday afternoons, which 
is in line with the current WPC forecast. As these waves pass 
through they will take with them small chunks of heat and moisture, 
gradually knocking our temperatures and humidity levels down into 
mid next week.

&&

.AVIATION /00Z WEDNESDAY THROUGH SUNDAY/...
Through 00Z Thursday...Changeable and variable conditions will
continue through the next 12 hours for most sites as a series of
boundaries moves through the entire area. Current radar shows
two areas of thunderstorms around both MSS and PBG. Have a
PROB30 with VCTS for both terminals for an hour at PBG, and
until 03Z at MSS with training thunderstorms along the US 11
corridor (Best chance of a terminal impacting thunderstorm is at
MSS). The MSS boundary will continue to drift east across SLK 
and into PBG/BTV between 04-07Z. Thunderstorm chances will 
decrease in time, but a few strikes in the vicinity of the 
terminals cannot be ruled out; thunderstorm chances will be 
about 20-40%. As the showers and thunderstorms move through
between 04-07Z, LLWS associated with a 40knot jet at 2000ft agl
will be present at RUT/MPV/EFK. The difficult part of this 
forecast will be how low ceilings get. With a combination of 
ongoing rain, and a developing inversion tonight, ceilings will
likely bounce between IFR and low MVFR beyond 04-06Z, 
particularly at SLK/MPV/EFK. BTV/PBG should have enough flow 
under the inversion to keep cigs above IFR. With added moisture,
some low lying fog will be possible due to more sheltering at 
SLK/MPV with IFR vsbys possible, mainly after 07Z. GLAMP
probabilities show 40-60% chance of IFR ceilings between 06-11Z
at SLK/MPV. Towards sunrise, conditions should gradually 
improve towards VFR. A few light showers will be possible at 
MSS/MPV/EFK, but terminal impacts are not expected. A boundary 
will cross the region tomorrow morning turning winds to the west
southwest.

Outlook...

Wednesday Night: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Thursday: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Thursday Night: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Friday: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Friday Night: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Saturday: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Saturday Night: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Sunday: VFR. Chance SHRA, Slight chance TSRA.

&&

.MARINE...
As of 209 PM EDT Tuesday...

A Lake Wind Advisory remains in effect with sustained 
south/southeast winds 20 to 35 knots and waves in the 2-4 foot 
range. Winds will peak late this afternoon/early this evening, then 
diminish to 15 to 25 knots overnight with waves 1 to 3 feet. 
Wednesday, winds will decrease to under 20 knots and the Lake 
Wind Advisory will likely be able to be dropped early in the 
day. 

In addition to the strong background winds, showers with some 
embedded thunderstorms are expected late this afternoon into this 
evening.  Within these showers and thunderstorms, gusty winds may 
briefly change directions and intensify.  Some cloud to ground 
lightning is also possible.

&&

.BTV WATCHES/WARNINGS/ADVISORIES...
VT...None.
NY...None.

&&

$$

WHAT HAS CHANGED...Duell
DISCUSSION...Duell/Danzig
AVIATION...Danzig
MARINE...NWS BTV

air.discussion: On Wednesday, July 22nd, expect a chance for some low-moderate fine particulate matter pollution in Vermont as residual wildfire smoke continues to circulate in the northeast US and Canada.
